steps.title: Find Kamtekar's key contrast
steps.content:
The ethicists claim situations override character, so character is meaningless.

Kamtekar responds by defining what we actually expect from character in our everyday thinking: helpful character should produce frequent helping, not [blank].

This contrast reveals what standard she's defending.

steps.title: Match the meaning to the choices
steps.content:
Unfailingly is the right opposite. Kamtekar acknowledges that character is not “unfailing”—situations do sometimes override it.

But character is still meaningful because it predicts frequent behavior most of the time.

This defends character without pretending it's a perfect override of all circumstances.

By rejecting the ethicists' impossible standard (unfailing) in favor of a modest one (frequent), she shows character remains relevant even though situations matter.

A and C address motivation and attitude, which miss the argument; B focuses only on frequency without engaging the deeper philosophical point about what standard character actually needs to meet.

This matches choice D — D is correct.
